This slide displays a Terraform configuration for a Google Compute Engine instance, highlighting the boot disk setup with a Debian 9 image and network interface configuration using the "default" network. Key points include specifying the OS image for the boot disk and defining the network settings for the instance.

chunks.text:
[Scene] This slide displays Google Cloud instance configuration code in a terminal, focusing on network interface settings (name, network/subnet URLs, IPs), access configuration (NAT IP, PREMIUM network tier), and scheduling options (automatic restart, host maintenance behavior, non - preemptible status).

[OCR Text] PROBLEMS OUTPUT DEBUG CONSOLE TERMINAL Gg bash +

network_interface {

name = “nico”

network = "https://www. googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/fire
lobal/networks/default"

network_ip = "10.128.0.6"

subnetwork = "https://www. googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/fire
egions/us-central1/subnetworks/default”

subnetwork_project = "fireship-lessons”

access config {

nat_ip "35.184.156.31"

network_tier = “PREMIUM”
}
}
scheduling {
automatic_restart = true
on_host_maintenance = “MIGRATE”
preemptible = false

Terraform used the selected providers to generate the following execution plan. R
actions are indicated with the following symbols:
- destroy

# google _compute_instance.default will be destroyed

- deletion_protection = false -> null

- enable_display = false -> null

- guest_accelerator = [] -> null

- id = "projects/fireship-lessons/zones/us-centrall-a/ins
-> null

[Scene] This slide explains a **Distributed Message Queue** architecture, where publishers send messages through "Publish Forwarders" to multiple consumers (represented by colored shapes), illustrating the flow of message distribution. A sidebar also promotes "System Design Interview" resources for learning common interview questions.
